title: Death of Valentinian.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Sarmatæ after this having made incursions into the Roman territories, the emperor marched against them with a numerous army but when the barbarians understood the formidable nature of this expedition, they sent an embassy to him to sue for peace on certain conditions.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As the ambassadors were introduced to the emperor’s presence, and appeared to him to be not very dignified fellows, he enquired whether all the Sarmatæ were such as these?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As they replied that the noblest personages of their whole nation had come to him, Valentinian became excessively enraged, and exclaimed with great vehemence, that ‘the Roman empire was indeed most wretched in devolving upon him at a time when a nation of such despicable barbarians, not content with being permitted to exist in safety within their own limits, dared to take up arms, invade the Roman territories, and break forth into open war.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The violence of his manner and utterance of these words was so great, that all his veins were opened by the effort, and all the arteries ruptured; and from the quantity of blood which thereupon gushed forth he died.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This occurred at Bergition Castle, after Gratian’s third consulate in conjunction with Equitius, on the seventeenth day of November, Valentinian having lived fifty-four years and reigned thirteen.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Upon the decease of Valentinian, six days after his death the army in Italy proclaimed his son Valentinian, then a young child, emperor, at Acincum, a city of Italy.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When this was announced to the other two emperors, they were displeased, not because the brother of the one and the nephew of the other had been declared emperor, but because the military presumed to proclaim him without consulting them, whom they themselves wished to have proclaimed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They both, however, ratified the transaction, and thus was Valentinian the younger seated on his father’s throne.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Now this Valentinian was born of Justina, whom Valentinian the elder married while Severa his former wife was alive, under the following circumstances.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Justus the father of Justina, who had been governor of Picenum under the reign of Constantius, had a dream in which he seemed to himself to bring forth the imperial purple out of his right side.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When this dream had been told to many persons, it at length came to the knowledge of Constantius, who conjecturing it to be a presage that a descendant of Justus would become emperor, caused him to be assassinated.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Justina being thus bereft of her father, still continued a virgin.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Some time after she became known to Severa, wife of the emperor Valentinian, and had frequent intercourse with the empress, until their intimacy at length grew to such an extent that they were accustomed to bathe together.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When Severa saw Justina in the bath she was greatly struck with the beauty of the virgin, and spoke of her to the emperor; saying that the daughter of Justus was so lovely a creature, and possessed of such symmetry of form, that she herself, though a woman, was altogether charmed with her.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor, treasuring this description by his wife in his own mind, considered with himself how he could espouse Justina, without repudiating Severa, as she had borne him Gratian, whom he had created Augustus a little while before.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He accordingly framed a law, and caused it to be published throughout all the cities, by which any man was permitted to have two lawful wives.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The law was promulgated and he married Justina, by whom he had Valentinian the younger, and three daughters, Justa, Grata, and Galla; the two former of these remained virgins: but Calla was afterwards married to the emperor Theodosius the Great, who had by her a daughter named Placidia.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For that prince had Arcadius and Honorius by Flaccilla his former wife: we shall however enter into particulars respecting Theodosius and his sons in the proper place.
